claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit); Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let me help you check your Child Benefit eligibility. 

To start, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for:

**How old is your child?**

Please tell me if they are:
- Under 16 years old
- Between 16-19 years old
- 20 years old or over

This helps determine the basic age qualification for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Yes, I'd like to check if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex, who's 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Thank you for that information. Let me check the eligibility for your child Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Since Alex is 8 years old (under 16), they meet the basic age requirement. Now I need to ask a few more questions:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

(For an 8-year-old, this would typically be "yes" as they're not in paid work, but I need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh yes, Alex doesn't work at all - he's only 8! He's still in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, that makes sense! Now the next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

In other words, Alex is not claiming benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in his own name, correct?

(Again, for an 8-year-old in primary school, this would typically not apply, but I need to confirm to continue the assessment.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name - he's just a kid!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now the next check:

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

(Since Alex is 8 years old and in primary school, I expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm this. Certain types of apprenticeships can affect eligibility for older children.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in an apprenticeship - he's only 8 and in primary school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we need to check your residency status. This is an important requirement for Child Benefit.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **ANY** of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself and family
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these apply to you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! You meet the residency requirement. Now the next question:

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You're considered responsible if **ANY** of these apply:

1. **You live with Alex**
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care - things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents)
3. You're paying maintenance under a court order or agreement

**Do any of these apply to you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! You're responsible for Alex as you live together. Now I need to check:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Another family member

If someone else is claiming, only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child at a time, though you can transfer the claim if all parties agree.

**Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check Alex's living situation:

**Which of the following best describes Alex's current living arrangement?**

- Is Alex currently in hospital?
- Is Alex in local authority care?
- Is Alex being fostered?
- Or is Alex living normally at home with you?

Please let me know which applies.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, Alex has been in hospital for a while now - about a couple of months.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I see. I'm sorry to hear Alex has been in hospital. Let me check how this affects eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. The hospital stay is an important factor. Let me ask you some specific questions about this:

**How long has Alex been in hospital in total?**

You mentioned it's been about a couple of months. To be precise:
- Has it been less than 12 weeks total, or
- Has it been more than 12 weeks?

Also, I need to know: **Is this a continuous hospital stay, or has Alex been in and out of hospital?**

This matters because there's a cumulative rule: if Alex has had multiple hospital stays with gaps of less than 28 days between them, those stays are added together when counting the time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been about 11 weeks now, all in one continuous stay.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks (which is under the 12-week limit), Child Benefit can continue. However, I need to ask an important question:

**Are you regularly spending money on Alex while he is in hospital?**

This could include things like:
- Buying clothes, toys, or treats for him
- Travel costs to visit him
- Other expenses for his care or comfort

This is important because if the hospital stay does exceed 12 weeks, you can still claim Child Benefit if you're regularly spending money on Alex's needs.

(Note: If you have a spouse or civil partner living with you, their spending on Alex also counts.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I still buy things for him - clothes, magazines, his favorite snacks, that sort of thing.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! That's good to know. Now I have one final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, the local council is not covering the costs of Alex's care or accommodation, correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, the council isn't paying anything towards his care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit

Based on all the information you've provided, **you meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for Alex**.

### Why You're Eligible:

✅ **Alex meets the child requirements:**
- He's 8 years old (under 16)
- Not in paid work
- Not receiving benefits in his own right
- Not in an apprenticeship

✅ **You meet the claimant requirements:**
- You normally live in the UK (Manchester)
- You're responsible for Alex (living with him)
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him

✅ **Hospital stay is covered:**
- Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks, which is under the 12-week limit
- You continue to regularly spend money on him (clothes, magazines, snacks)
- The local council is not paying for his care

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge
5. **Payments:** Child Benefit is usually paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

**Apply here:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

I hope Alex recovers soon! If you have any other questions, feel free to ask.

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) Alex is 8 years old, which is under 16 and meets the age requirement; (2) Alex is not in paid work; (3) Alex is not receiving benefits in his own right; (4) Alex is not in an apprenticeship; (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements; (6) The claimant lives with Alex and is responsible for him; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Although Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks, this is under the 12-week limit, and the claimant continues to regularly spend money on Alex while he is in hospital; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 8-year-old son Alex. Despite Alex's current hospital stay of 11 weeks, Child Benefit eligibility is maintained because the stay is under the 12-week limit and the claimant continues to regularly spend money on Alex's needs during his hospitalization.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) Alex is 8 years old, which is under 16 and meets the age requirement; (2) Alex is not in paid work; (3) Alex is not receiving benefits in his own right; (4) Alex is not in an apprenticeship; (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements; (6) The claimant lives with Alex and is responsible for him; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Although Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks, this is under the 12-week limit, and the claimant continues to regularly spend money on Alex while he is in hospital; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 8-year-old son Alex. Despite Alex's current hospital stay of 11 weeks, Child Benefit eligibility is maintained because the stay is under the 12-week limit and the claimant continues to regularly spend money on Alex's needs during his hospitalization.
